role_summary: Korean War veteran; Esau Jenkins mentee in the Johns Island Progressive Club (one of the first Citizenship Schools in the network Septima Clark and the Highlander Folk School built across the South); chief organizer and lead negotiator of the March-June 1969 Charleston Hospital Workers Strike (the 113-day strike at the Medical College of South Carolina that drew Ralph Abernathy, Coretta Scott King, and the SCLC to Charleston); founder of the Committee on Better Racial Assurance (COBRA, 1970); operator of WPAL-AM radio in Charleston (1972-1998).
ai_reading: Saunders's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 80), with Charles McLaurin (entry 17) as the top match and the broader cluster including James Oscar Jones (entry 57), John Churchville (entry 63), and Walter Bruce (entry 123). The novel observation his page surfaces is the Lowcountry-and-Mississippi cross-region pairing: the catalog's strongest cosine for Saunders is McLaurin (Mississippi voter-registration), not the other South Carolina Lowcountry catalog voices the cultural-historical record would predict pairing him with; the embedding catches the Black-Power-era community-organizing register Saunders developed at Highlander Folk School as continuous with the SNCC Mississippi field tradition rather than as a separately-anchored Charleston tradition.
biographical_paragraph: While Saunders's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is well-documented in the College of Charleston Lowcountry Digital History Initiative archive [src: 1, src: 2]. Saunders was born in 1935 in New York City and raised on Johns Island, South Carolina. At sixteen he enlisted in the U.S. Army and served in the Korean War. Upon returning to Johns Island he became involved in the Progressive Club, led by his mentor Esau Jenkins; the Progressive Club was one of the first nodes in the Citizenship Schools network that Septima Poinsette Clark (FamousNames catalog page) and the Highlander Folk School built across the South to train rural African Americans in literacy and voter-registration preparation [src: 2]. Saunders himself attended the Highlander Folk School in Tennessee in the early 1960s. He worked at a Charleston mattress factory through the decade. In March-June 1969 he served as chief organizer and lead negotiator of the Charleston Hospital Workers Strike at the Medical College of South Carolina, the 113-day strike that drew Ralph Abernathy and Coretta Scott King to Charleston in support and that ended with a negotiated settlement between Saunders and MCH vice president William Huff [src: 2]. In 1970 he established the Committee on Better Racial Assurance (COBRA) to address race-related community problems in Charleston; from 1972 to 1998 he operated WPAL-AM radio in the city; he founded the Lowcountry Newsletter and ran unsuccessfully for the South Carolina state senate [src: 2].
